name: Flint Hand Axe
description: A teardrop-shaped tool knapped from flint by early humans. Hand axes were the Swiss Army knife of the Stone Age — used for butchering, digging and scraping.
funFact: Acheulean hand axes appear almost unchanged across 1.5 million years of human history, making them the longest-used tool ever.

name: Bone Needle
description: A delicate sewing needle carved from animal bone with a tiny drilled eye. Its discovery proved that early humans wore tailored clothing — essential for surviving ice age winters.
funFact: Bone needles this fine required sophisticated tools to make. Whoever crafted this was as skilled as any modern craftsperson.

name: Neolithic Pot Shard
description: A fragment of hand-coiled pottery decorated with finger-pressed patterns. The invention of pottery changed everything — food could now be stored, boiled and fermented.
funFact: Archaeologists can identify a settlement's age, trade routes and diet just from broken pot sherds like this one.

name: Bronze Fibula
description: An ornate bronze safety pin used to fasten cloaks and tunics. Fibulae were status symbols — the more elaborate the design, the higher the wearer's rank in Celtic society.
funFact: Fibulae are one of the most common Iron Age finds, yet no two are exactly alike — each was individually handcrafted.

name: Roman Denarius
description: A silver coin stamped with the profile of a Roman emperor. Denarii were the standard pay for a Roman soldier — one coin per day for a legionary on campaign.
funFact: Roman coins have been found as far as India and China, evidence of a trade network stretching across three continents.

name: Obsidian Arrowhead
description: A razor-sharp projectile point knapped from volcanic obsidian glass. Obsidian fractures so cleanly that ancient arrowheads can be sharper than modern surgical steel.
funFact: By tracing obsidian to its volcanic source, archaeologists can map prehistoric trade routes across hundreds of kilometres.

name: Viking Silver Brooch
description: A heavy silver brooch in the Borre style, used to pin a cloak at the shoulder. Viking brooches often doubled as portable wealth — if you needed silver, you simply cut a piece off.
funFact: Many Viking brooches were made from melted-down foreign coins, blending Arabic, Byzantine and Anglo-Saxon silver into Norse art.

name: Egyptian Scarab Seal
description: A carved stone amulet in the shape of a dung beetle, with hieroglyphs on the flat side used as a personal seal. Scarabs were believed to channel the sun god Khepri's power of rebirth.
funFact: Scarabs were so popular they were exported across the ancient Mediterranean — Egyptian scarabs have been found in Greek, Phoenician and Sardinian tombs.

name: Golden Torc
description: A twisted gold neck ring worn by Celtic warriors and chieftains. Torcs were among the most prized possessions in the Celtic world — symbols of divine favour and battlefield courage.
funFact: The Snettisham Great Torc, found in Norfolk, contains nearly 1 kg of twisted gold and electrum alloy.

name: Cuneiform Tablet
description: A small clay tablet impressed with wedge-shaped cuneiform script — one of humanity's earliest writing systems. Most tablets recorded grain inventories, debts and trade contracts.
funFact: One cuneiform tablet from 1750 BCE is the world's oldest customer complaint — a merchant furiously writing about bad copper ingots.

name: Iron Dagger
description: A short double-edged iron blade with a bone handle. The spread of iron-working around 1200 BCE transformed warfare — iron weapons were stronger and far cheaper to produce than bronze.
funFact: The oldest iron ever worked by humans came from meteorites — early smiths called iron the 'metal of heaven'.

name: Human Femur
description: A well-preserved human thigh bone from an ancient burial site. Forensic analysis can reveal the individual's age, sex, diet, diseases and even the kind of work they did in life.
funFact: Cut marks on ancient human bones often reveal burial rituals — some cultures defleshed bones as a sacred act of respect.

name: Charred Wooden Post
description: A charred structural post from a prehistoric roundhouse or palisade. Charring actually preserves wood by preventing rot — some posts have survived 4,000 years underground.
funFact: Dendrochronology (tree-ring dating) on preserved posts can pinpoint the exact year a tree was felled — sometimes down to the season.

name: Ivory Bead Necklace
description: Dozens of tiny beads carved from mammoth ivory, originally strung as a necklace. Personal ornaments this old prove that symbolic thinking and identity existed long before writing.
funFact: Shell beads found in Morocco date to 130,000 BCE — making jewellery one of the oldest human behaviours ever recorded.

name: Mammoth Molar
description: A ridged, washboard-sized tooth from a woolly mammoth. The parallel enamel plates ground up tough grasses and shrubs as the animal roamed the ice-age steppe.
funFact: A single mammoth went through six sets of molars in its lifetime — when the last set wore out, the animal starved.

name: Glass Trade Bead
description: A small bead of coloured glass, one of thousands produced in Venice and traded across three continents. Strings of these beads served as currency on long-distance routes.
funFact: Venetian chevron beads made in the 15th century have been found in graves from Canada to Zimbabwe — the world's first truly globalised product.

name: Bone Gaming Die
description: A six-sided die carved from bone, with dots drilled and filled with pigment. Romans gambled obsessively — dice games were banned in public but flourished in every tavern.
funFact: Some Roman dice are demonstrably loaded — weighted on one side to favour a particular roll. Cheating is almost as old as gaming itself.

name: Hand-Forged Nail
description: A square-shanked iron nail, hand-hammered to a point on a blacksmith's anvil. Before machine production, every nail in a building was individually forged — a slow, expensive process.
funFact: When medieval buildings were abandoned, they were often burned down purely to recover the nails — iron was that valuable.

name: Clay Loom Weight
description: A perforated clay disc used to tension warp threads on a vertical loom. Found in clusters wherever households once wove cloth, these weights are fingerprints of domestic life.
funFact: Some loom weights carry finger impressions so crisp that archaeologists can estimate the age and sex of the weaver who shaped them 2,000 years ago.

name: Medieval Horseshoe
description: A crescent of iron shaped to a horse's hoof, with square nail holes punched along the rim. The nailed iron shoe transformed medieval warfare, farming and long-distance travel.
funFact: Archaeologists can often date a site just from horseshoe styles — the shape of the shoe evolved almost every century.

name: Grinding Stone
description: A worn saddle-shaped grinding stone used to crush grain into flour. The surface is polished smooth from countless hours of back-breaking work — usually by women.
funFact: Skeletons of Neolithic women show distinctive arthritis in the toes, knees and spine from kneeling to grind grain for hours every day.

name: Bronze Mirror
description: A polished bronze disc with a decorated back, used to reflect a lamplit face. Celtic mirrors feature some of the most intricate La Tène curvilinear art ever recovered.
funFact: Because bronze tarnishes quickly, ancient mirrors had to be repolished constantly — a reflection in one was fleeting and rare.

name: Baltic Amber Pendant
description: A honey-coloured lump of fossilised tree resin, drilled for suspension on a cord. Amber from the Baltic coast was traded as far as Egypt — the 'gold of the north'.
funFact: When rubbed, amber generates static electricity — the Greek word for amber (ēlektron) is the root of the modern word 'electricity'.

name: Medieval Seal Matrix
description: A bronze stamp engraved with a family crest, pressed into hot wax to authenticate letters and charters. Seals were the medieval equivalent of a signature and password combined.
funFact: Losing your seal matrix was a catastrophe — forgers could use it to sign away your land. Owners often smashed or buried old seals when a new one was made.

name: Pattern-Welded Sword
description: A corroded iron blade forged by twisting and folding rods of different steels, producing a rippling grain on the surface. Each sword was unique — a lifetime's work for a master smith.
funFact: Pattern-welded blades were so prized they were given names and passed down for generations. The sword was often considered a member of the family.

name: Black-Figure Vase Shard
description: A curved fragment of a Greek amphora, painted with black silhouettes of warriors on an orange-red ground. Details were incised through the glaze with a sharp point.
funFact: Attic potters often signed their work — we know the names of individual Greek artists from 2,500 years ago, something true of almost no other ancient craft.

name: Gladiator's Helmet
description: A bronze helmet with a high crest and a latticed visor, worn by a murmillo — a heavy-armoured gladiator fighting with sword and shield. Only a few dozen survive worldwide.
funFact: Gladiator helmets weighed up to 7 kg — so heavy that fights rarely lasted more than 10 minutes before one combatant collapsed from exhaustion.

name: Shang Oracle Bone
description: An ox scapula scorched and cracked by a hot bronze rod, then inscribed with questions to the ancestors. The cracks were read as divine answers about harvests, battles and births.
funFact: Oracle bones preserve the earliest known form of Chinese writing. Every modern Chinese character traces back to marks scratched into bones like this one.

name: Mayan Jade Fragment
description: A carved tile of green jade from a mosaic burial mask. Maya kings were interred wearing masks of hundreds of jade pieces, transforming the dead ruler's face into that of a god.
funFact: Jade was more precious than gold to the Maya. When the Spanish arrived demanding gold, Maya rulers were baffled that anyone would ignore the real treasure.

name: Iridescent Prism
description: A flawless crystal that splits sunlight into colours that have no name. Every spectrometer that examines it produces a different reading. No one knows where it came from or how it formed.
funFact: Reportedly, no two photographs of an Iridescent Prism ever look the same — even taken seconds apart, in the same light.
